coredata.dc:title: MnO2 nanowires tuning of photoluminescence of alloy Cu/Ag NCs and thiamine enables a ratiometric fluorescent sensing of glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xue
Liu, Xidong
Zhang, Xiaodan
Cao, Haiyan
Huang, Yuming
coredata.dc:description: By tuning the photoluminescence of alloy Cu/Ag nanoclusters (NCs) and thiamine (VB1) simultaneously through MnO2 nanowires, we report a simple and label-free ratiometric fluorescent platform for sensitively and selectively sensing of glutathione (GSH). Ag doping of Cu NCs significantly enhances the quantum yield (QY, about 2-fold enhancement), oxidation resistance to air, storage stability (60 days versus 1 day), photostability and salt stability of the resultant Cu/Ag NCs. Impressively, MnO2 nanowires quench the fluorescence of Cu/Ag NCs, while they enhance the fluorescence of nonfluorescent VB1 via oxidation reaction between VB1 and dissolved oxygen to produce oxVB1 as a nanozyme catalyst. Upon introduction of GSH as a target, the MnO2 nanowires are reduced to Mn2+ and lose their quenching capacity and oxidase-like activity, resulting in FL recovery of Cu/Ag NCs and fluorescent decrease of oxVB1. Hence, a ratiometric luminescence signal is obtained, providing a novel ratiometric fluorescent strategy for detecting GSH through target induced regulation of the catalytic activities and quenching ability of MnO2 nanowires. The sensor exhibited a wide linear concentration range (10 nM to 70 μM) for GSH with a detection limit of 6.5 nM. The ratiometric sensor was successfully used for GSH detection in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensors
Cu/Ag nanoclusters
MnO2 nanowires
Glutathione
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Cu/Ag NCs and Cu NCs
General procedure for ratiometric fluorescent analysis of GSH
Procedure for GSH detection in human serum samples and glutathione tablets
Synthesis and characterization of Cu/Ag NCs and MnO2 nanowires
The principle of ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GSH
Sensitive and selective detection of GSH
Detection of GSH in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GSH based on MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system.

(A) Emission spectra of Cu/Ag NCs (black line) and Cu NCs (red line). (B) TEM image of Cu/Ag NCs. Inset: HRTEM image of Cu/Ag NCs. (C) XPS spectrum of Cu in Cu/Ag NCs. (D) XPS spectrum of Ag in Cu/Ag NCs (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system in the presence of different dosage of MnO2. (B) Plots of the fluorescence intensity of Cu/Ag NCs (F 560), oxVB1 (F 410), and the ratio value F 560/F 410 versus concentrations of MnO2. The error bars represent one standard deviation based on three independent measurements.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system in the presence of different concentrations of GSH from 0 to 500 μM. (B) Plots of the ratio value F 560/F 410 versus concentrations of GSH. (C) The linear relationship between the ratio value F 560/F 410 and GSH concentrations from 0.01 to 70 μM. (D) Fluorescence response of MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system in the presence of foreign substances. The concentrations of various metal ions and alanine (Ala), phenylalanine (Phe), tyrosine (Tyr), threonine (Thr), serine (Ser), proline (Pro), methionine (Met), glucose (Glu) and fructose (Fru) are all 1 mM; the concentrations of cysteine (Cys) and ascorbic acid (AA) are 10 μM. In addition, the concentration of GSH is 100 μM. The error bars represent one standard deviation based on three independent measurements.

head.ce:title: MnO2 nanowires tuning of photoluminescence of alloy Cu/Ag NCs and thiamine enables a ratiometric fluorescent sensing of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-time Analytical Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences, Chongqing, 402160, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering
Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Chongqing Inorganic Special Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangtze Normal University, Chongqing, 408100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Chongqing Inorganic Special Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By tuning the photoluminescence of Cu/Ag NCs and thiamine (VB1) simultaneously through bifunctional MnO2 acting as both a nanozyme catalyst and fluorescence quencher, we report a simple and label-free ratiometric fluorescent platform for sensitively and selectively detecting GSH in biological samples.

By tuning the photoluminescence of alloy Cu/Ag nanoclusters (NCs) and thiamine (VB1) simultaneously through MnO2 nanowires, we report a simple and label-free ratiometric fluorescent platform for sensitively and selectively sensing of glutathione (GSH). Ag doping of Cu NCs significantly enhances the quantum yield (QY, about 2-fold enhancement), oxidation resistance to air, storage stability (60 days versus 1 day), photostability and salt stability of the resultant Cu/Ag NCs. Impressively, MnO2 nanowires quench the fluorescence of Cu/Ag NCs, while they enhance the fluorescence of nonfluorescent VB1 via oxidation reaction between VB1 and dissolved oxygen to produce oxVB1 as a nanozyme catalyst. Upon introduction of GSH as a target, the MnO2 nanowires are reduced to Mn2+ and lose their quenching capacity and oxidase-like activity, resulting in FL recovery of Cu/Ag NCs and fluorescent decrease of oxVB1. Hence, a ratiometric luminescence signal is obtained, providing a novel ratiometric fluorescent strategy for detecting GSH through target induced regulation of the catalytic activities and quenching ability of MnO2 nanowires. The sensor exhibited a wide linear concentration range (10 nM to 70 μM) for GSH with a detection limit of 6.5 nM. The ratiometric sensor was successfully used for GSH detection in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescent sensors have drawn considerable attention in analytical sensing owing to their minimizing the interference from background factors by providing a built-in correction for environmental effects [1,2]. Up to now, a variety of ratiometric fluorescent sensors have been developed based on naphthalimide [3], nitroolefin-based BODIPY [4], sulfonamide derivative [5], dye-modified Au nanoparticles [6], and quantum dots/Au nanoclusters (NCs) hybrid [7]. However, to construct these ratiometric sensors, the dual emission materials were needed with cumbersome and complex modification process, which limited their further applications [8,9]. Therefore, fabrication of a simple and label-free ratiometric fluorescent (FL) sensor is of high demand for further study.

Recently, some nanomaterials, particularly 2D nanomaterials, such as MnO2 [10,11], CoOOH [12] and MoS2 nanosheets [13], have attracted growing research interest in biosensing due to their versatility, better functionality, and unique electronic property [13–18]. On the one hand, these nanomaterials could work as oxidase mimics to directly catalyze the oxidation of substrates by molecular oxygen, which could be used for construction of turn-off sensors based on target-suppressed the oxidase-like activity [10–12,19]. On the other hand, they could also work as quencher of fluorescent substances via inner filter effect (IFE) [20,21], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [22–25], dynamic quenching effect (DQE) [26] and static quenching effect (SQE) [20,26], which could be used for design of turn-on fluorescent sensors based on target-suppressed FL quenching [22–27]. Unfortunately, these sensors are single-signal responsive, and their applications are restricted by the background noises derived from sample or environment [28]. To address this issue, Dong’s group developed the MnO2 nanosheets-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for glutathione (GSH) based on the oxidase-like property of MnO2 nanosheets [29]. In this sensor, MnO2 nanosheets could enhance the fluorescence of Amplex Red (AR) and quench the fluorescence of Scopoletin (SC) simultaneously by oxidation reaction, thus realizing ratiometric fluorescent sensing and largely minimizing the interference from environment and improving the sensitivity of sensor. Unfortunately, dual-wavelength excitation mode (excited at 380 nm for SC and excited at 560 nm for AR) was used to obtain the dual fluorescent signals for developing the ratiometric sensor, making the detection process complicated. Furthermore, the fluorescent dye AR is easy to photooxidation [30], making the ratiometric sensor instable and hampering its further applications in biosensing.

Copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) have received great interest due to their discrete electronic states and strong emission [31,32]. In particular, thiolate-protected Cu NCs (thiolate-Cu NCs) exhibited unique aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property [33], which are appropriate fluorescent probes in biosensing and bioimaging due to their nontoxic, biocompatibility, water-soluble, long Stokes shift and facile synthesis [34–36]. However, most of the reported thiolate-Cu NCs have short shelf lives as they are easily oxidized when exposed in air [37], which limits their further applications in biosensing. Recently, metal doping is demonstrated to be an efficient way to improve the properties of nanodots, such as carbon dots [38], semiconductor quantum dots [39], and metal nanoclusters [40]. Although there are some examples of applications of alloy nanoclusters [41–45], there is a lack of facile and effective strategies to prepare alloy nanoclusters with excellent stability and high quantum yield in an aqueous media, which could be beneficial for promoting analytical applications of Cu NCs. Here, we proposed a simple and facile strategy to synthesize the D-penicillamine (DPA)-Cu/Ag NCs by introducing Ag during the aggregating process of Cu NCs, and this strategy significantly enhanced the fluorescence response and stability of the resultant alloy Cu/Ag NCs, i.e. about 2-fold enhancement in quantum yield (QY), oxidation resistance to air, storage stability (60 days versus 1 day), good photostability and salt stability in an aqueous media.

Different from previous fluorescent quenching systems, in this work, by using a single-wavelength excitation mode and highly stable alloy Cu/Ag NCs, we developed a simple, label-free and stable ratiometric sensor for sensitive and selective detection of GSH. GSH was selected as target because it is a predominant nonprotein thiol in the mammalian cells, playing a significant role in biological systems, such as detoxification [46], radical scavenging [47], and maintenance of metabolism of cellular [48]. Meanwhile, the levels of GSH have been demonstrated to be related to numerous diseases, such as diabetes [49], liver damage [50], cancer [51], Alzheimer [52], and so on. Thus, the quantitative determination of GSH in living systems is essential for clinical diagnosis. The working principle of the ratiometric fluorescent platform for GSH is schematically depicted in Scheme 1 . As shown in the scheme, MnO2 nanowires quench the fluorescence of Cu/Ag NCs through IFE, while they enhance the fluorescence of nonfluorescent thiamine (VB1) via it oxidation by dissolved oxygen to produce oxVB1 as a nanozyme catalyst. Impressively, using a single-wavelength excitation mode produces the dual fluorescent signals, namely, FL emission of oxVB1 at 410 nm and Cu/Ag NCs at 560 nm can be simultaneously acquired by a single-wavelength excitation at 330 nm. Upon introduction of GSH as a target, the MnO2 nanowires are reduced to Mn2+ and lose their quenching capacity and oxidase-like activity, resulting in FL recovery of Cu/Ag NCs and FL decrease of oxVB1. Hence, by tuning the photoluminescence of Cu/Ag NCs and VB1 simultaneously through MnO2 nanowires, a ratiometric luminescence signal is obtained, which provides a new ratiometric fluorescent strategy for detecting GSH. The use of single-wavelength excitation mode, highly fluorescent and highly stable Cu/Ag NCs makes the constructed ratiometric FL sensor much more simple and stable than previously reported one [29]. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) for GSH is 6.5 nM, which is the lowest among the MnO2-based (Table S1) and nanoclusters-based (Table S2) fluorescent GSH sensor reported so far. The developed ratiometric FL sensor was successfully used for the detection of GSH in human serum samples. To our knowledge, the alloy Cu/Ag NCs have not been exploited yet to construct ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GSH sensing.

DPA, cysteine (Cys) and GSH were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI, with molecular weight of 2500) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, Sigma-Aldrich, China). KMnO4 was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Glucose and fructose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). CuSO4·5H2O, AgNO3, VB1, and other chemicals were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagents Company (Sichuan, China). All regents are of analytical grade and used directly. All solutions were prepared by ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ ⋅ cm).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Absorption measurements were taken on a U-4100 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The pH of the solution was measured by a Mettler Toledo FE20 pH meter (Mettler-Toledo Instruments Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Talos F200S (FEI, USA). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out on a S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on a Bruker D8 Advance powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were recorded by a VG Multilab 2000X instrument (Thermal Electron, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Cu/Ag NCs and Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of Cu/Ag NCs, 0.25 mL of CuSO4·5H2O solution (0.1 M) was added in a beaker with 9.5 mL of water, and then 0.0372 g of DPA was added under stirring. After the white emulsion was formed, 0.25 mL of AgNO3 solution (0.1 M) and 0.0186 g of DPA were added in order, and the mixture was stirred for 1 h under ambient temperature to obtain Cu/Ag NCs. The solution was then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 500 Da) in water for 48 h to remove the unreacted species. The prepared Cu/Ag NCs were diluted to 20 mL and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

Cu NCs were prepared as follows: 0.25 mL of CuSO4·5H2O solution (0.1 M) was added in a beaker with 9.75 mL water, and then 0.0372 g of DPA was added under stirring. Finally, the mixture was stirred for 1.5 h under room temperature. The obtained Cu NCs were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

The MnO2 nanowires were prepared facilely by hydrothermal method based on the previous report [53]. First, 0.79 g KMnO4 was dissolved in 25 mL water, and 25 mL of BPEI aqueous solution (0.25 wt%) was added quickly under vigorous stirring. After 20 min reaction, the brown mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. Finally, the product was filtered by 0.45 μm membranes, washed 3 times by ultra-pure water and ethanol in order, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for ratiometric fluorescent analysis of GSH
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, 0.5 mL of GSH solution (with different final concentrations from 0.01 μM to 500 μM), 0.5 mL of VB1 (0.01 M), 0.4 mL of MnO2 nanowires (1 mg/mL), and 2 mL of HAc-NAc (0.2 M, pH 3.50) were added into 10 mL test tube in turn. After incubation for 10 min, 0.1 mL Cu/Ag NCs was added to the solution, and the solution was diluted to 5 mL with water. 15 min later, the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 410 nm and 560 nm was measured at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The ratio F560 /F410 versus GSH concentration was used for calibration. Here, F560 and F410 are the fluorescence intensity at 560 nm and 410 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for GSH detection in human serum samples and glutathione tablets
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum samples were filtrated by 30 kDa Amicon cell at 3000 rpm for 30 min, and diluted 100 times with water for GSH determination. The glutathione tablet containing 100 mg of GSH was grounded into powder, which was dissolved in 100 mL water. After filtration, aliquots of solution were diluted with water by a factor of 100. Then, 0.5 mL of the diluted solution was used for the ratiometric fluorescent measurement according to procedure in previous section. Also, the GSH content in human serum samples and glutathione tablets was analyzed by the standard o-phthalaldehyde (OPA)-based fluorescent method [54–56]. The detailed procedures were provided in Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Cu/Ag NCs and MnO2 nanowires
ce:section.ce:para: A facile and rapid one-pot method was used to synthesize Cu/Ag NCs. In the mixture of Cu2+ and DPA, the Ag+ was introduced to form Cu/Ag NCs. The prepared Cu/Ag NCs displayed strong orange fluorescence emission at 560 nm, which was different from the Cu NCs emitted at 625 nm (Fig. 1 A). This indicated that Ag doping caused the optical property change of the Cu NCs, which might be attributed to form a bimetal centre and change the state of excited charge [40]. The QY of Cu/Ag NCs (QY = 11.64%) was significantly higher than that of Cu NCs (QY = 6.29%). This might result from the metallophilic interaction of Cu and Ag, which leads to a ligand-to-Cu−Ag charge transfer that promotes the relaxation of excited electrons by a radiative pathway [40,57]. The TEM image demonstrated that the sole Cu NCs were in disperse state with the size of 1.66 ± 0.03 nm (Fig. S1) with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. S1), corresponding to the Cu(111) [58]. The as-prepared Cu/Ag NCs displayed a near-spherical structure with an average size of 1.9 nm and an aggregation states (Fig. 1B). The Cu/Ag NCs exhibited two lattice distances of 0.21 nm and 0.24 nm (inset in Fig. 1B), corresponding to Cu (111) plane [58] and Ag (111) plane [59], respectively. The presence of Cu (111) and Ag (111) in the Cu/Ag NCs was further confirmed by the XRD data. As shown in Fig. S2, the XRD patterns of the Cu/Ag NCs displayed specific diffraction peaks at 37.5° allocated to the Ag (111) plane [60] and at 43.7° related to Cu (111) plane [61]. Other diffraction peaks of Cu/Ag NCs were attributed to the “lamella reflections’’ of a multi-layered structure, which agreed with the previous report [62]. The XPS spectrum was used to confirm the element components of Cu/Ag NCs and the valence states of Cu and Ag. As shown in XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S3), Cu/Ag NCs were composed of Cu, Ag, C, N, O, and S. The atomic concentrations of Cu and Ag are 3.3% and 3.0% (Table S3), respectively. Thus, the elemental ratio of Cu to Ag is 1.1:1, which is very close to theoretical value of 1:1 (see Supplementary Material). The Cu 2p spectrum displays two peaks at 952.47 and 932.57 eV (Fig. 1C), representing Cu 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 respectively, which are agreement with Cu (I) and Cu (0) [63]. There is no peak at 942 eV, indicating the absence of Cu (II) in Cu/Ag NCs. In addition, the Ag 3d5/2 at 368 eV was observed in Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 1D), which is a characteristic peak of Ag (0) [64,65]. Above results confirm that the Cu/Ag NCs were synthesized successfully.

The MnO2 nanowires were synthesized by hydrothermal method according to previous report [53]. As shown in Fig. S4A, the prepared MnO2 displayed obvious wires structure. The diameter and length of the prepared MnO2 were about 14 nm and 700 nm, respectively. The TEM image further confirmed that the prepared MnO2 displayed nanowires structure (Fig. S4B). The prepared MnO2 displayed a broad UV absorption from 300 to 600 nm with a characteristic absorption at 380 nm (Fig. S5A). Furthermore, the XRD patterns display broad and weak peaks (Fig. S5B), which agrees well with the reference δ-MnO2 (JCPDS no.18–0802) [53].

The oxidation resistance, storage stability, photostability and salt stability of the Cu/Ag NCs were explored. The fluorescence response of Cu/Ag NCs maintained almost unchanged after 2 months (Fig. S6A), but that of Cu NCs almost disappeared completely after 7 days (Fig. S6B). The result suggested that Cu/Ag NCs possessed longer storage stability than Cu NCs. Also, compared with the Cu NCs, the Cu/Ag NCs exhibited good photostability (Fig. S6C). Finally, with concentration of NaCl increasing, the fluorescence response of Cu/Ag NCs displayed minor change, but that of Cu NCs gradually decreased (Fig. S6D). In addition, 100 μM of H2O2 could not induce the fluorescence change of Cu/Ag NCs, while it could quench 50% fluorescence emission of Cu NCs (Fig. S6E). Hence, the Cu/Ag NCs possessed excellent oxidation resistance, storage stability, photostability and salt stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle of ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GSH
ce:section.ce:para: VB1 is a non-fluorescent substance. It can generate enhanced fluorescence at 410 nm after H2O2 oxidation to produce oxVB1 [66]. Considering the oxidase-like activity of MnO2, it can catalyze the oxidation of VB1 without H2O2. As shown in Fig. S7A, in the absence of MnO2, VB1 presented a negligible signal at 410 nm. After adding MnO2, the signal at 410 nm was enhanced obviously. This is because MnO2 possesses oxidase-like activity [67], which functions as a nanozyme catalyst to directly catalyze the oxidation of VB1 by molecular oxygen to produce highly fluorescent oxVB1. On the other hand, the fluorescence of Cu/Ag NCs at 560 nm was quenched in the presence of MnO2 (Fig. S7A). This is because the broad absorption spectrum of the MnO2 from 200 nm to 600 nm has large overlap with the excitation spectra of the Cu/Ag NCs (Fig. S8A), resulting in an efficient IFE [20]. As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, the FL signal of the mixture of VB1 and Cu/Ag NCs depends on the concentration of MnO2. Specifically, the FL signal of oxVB1 at 410 nm increased gradually with increase in dosage of MnO2, while that of Cu/Ag NCs at 560 nm decreased gradually with increase in dosage of MnO2. This suggested that MnO2 could regulate the photoluminescence of Cu/Ag NCs and VB1 simultaneously. Therefore, a ratiometric luminescence signal was obtained, suggesting that the MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system can be used for developing a ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

MnO2 can be reduced to Mn2+ in the presence of GSH (MnO2 + 2GSH + 2H+ = Mn2+ + GSSG +2H2O) [21]. Thus, upon introduction of GSH into the MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system, the FL signal of oxVB1 at 410 nm decreased, and that of Cu/Ag NCs at 560 nm recovered (Fig. S7B). To further confirm the reaction between MnO2 and GSH, the UV absorbance of MnO2 in the absence and presence of GSH was measured. In the presence of GSH, the characteristic absorption of MnO2 at 380 nm decreased obviously (Fig. S8B), indicating that the MnO2 was reduced by GSH. Thus, the MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system can be used for FL ratiometric GSH sensing. Impressively, using a single-wavelength excitation mode produces the dual fluorescent signals, namely, FL emission of oxVB1 at 410 nm and Cu/Ag NCs at 560 nm can be simultaneously acquired by a single-wavelength excitation at 330 nm. Thus, compared with two-wavelength excitation mode [29], the proposed Cu/Ag NCs-based ratiometric FL GSH sensor makes the detection process simpler.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of GSH
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain a sensitive probe for the detection of GSH based on the MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system, the effects of reaction time and solution pH were investigated. As shown in Fig. S9A, in the absence of GSH, the reaction time had almost no effect on F560 /F410 value. However, in the presence of GSH, the F560 /F410 value increased gradually with increase in reaction time to 10 min, then maintained unchanged. Thus, 10 min was selected as optimized reaction time. The effect of solution pH ranging from 2 to 6 was investigated. As shown in Fig. S9B, in the absence of GSH, the F 560/F 410 value decreased with increase in solution pH to 3.0, then kept minor variance to 5.0, and further declined to pH 6.0. However, in the presence of GSH, the highest F 560/F 410 value was obtained at pH 3.5. When solution pH was higher or lower than 3.5, the F 560/F 410 value decreased. Thus, pH 3.5 was selected as the optimized pH value in this work. Under the optimized conditions, the MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs-VB1 system was used to detect GSH. As Fig. 3 A and B shown, with the concentration of GSH increasing from 10 nM to 500 μM, the fluorescence intensity at 410 nm decreased gradually, while that at 560 nm increased gradually. The ratiometric value of F 560/F 410 displayed a good linear relationship with the concentration of GSH from 10 nM to 70 μM (Fig. 3C). The linear equation was F 560/F 410 = 0.03362 [GSH]/μM + 0.3919 (n = 10, r 2 = 0.9954). The limit of detection (LOD, 3σ) for GSH was 6.5 nM, which was the lowest among the MnO2-based fluorescent GSH sensor (Table S1) and nanoclusters-based fluorescent GSH sensor (Table S2) reported so far. Hence, this ratiometric fluorescent sensor can realize sensitive detection of GSH. To illustrate the advantage of the current ratiometric sensor for GSH detection, two single-channel sensors for GSH detection based on MnO2-VB1 system and MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs system were compared. In the MnO2-VB1 system, the fluorescence response of VB1 at 410 nm was gradually decreased with increase in GSH concentration from 0 to 50 μM (Fig. S10 A). The fluorescence quenching value (F0 -F)/F0 (F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of MnO2-VB1 system without and with GSH, respectively.) displayed narrow linear relationship with the GSH concentration in the range from 0.1 to 1 μM (Fig. S10 B). The linear equation was (F0 -F)/F0 = 0.1407 [GSH]/μM + 0.05716 ( r 2 = 0.9914, n = 5). The LOD for GSH was 0.01 μM. In the MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs system, the fluorescence intensity of Cu/Ag NCs at 560 nm was gradually increased with increase in GSH concentration from 0 μM to 500 μM (Fig. S11 A). The fluorescence enhancement value (F-F0 )/F0 (F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of MnO2-Cu/Ag NCs system without and with GSH, respectively.) displayed linear relationship with the GSH concentrations in the range from 0.1 to 70 μM with linear equation of (F-F0 )/F0 = 0.01297 [GSH]/μM + 0.08962 ( r 2 = 0.9983, n = 7) (Fig. S11B). The LOD for GSH was 0.1 μM. It is clear that, compared with two single channel sensors, the proposed ratiometric sensor for GSH detection possessed lower LOD (6.5 nM) and wider detection range (10 nM to 70 μM), suggesting that the proposed ratiometric sensor displayed excellent performance for GSH sensing.

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed sensor, the effects of some common ions and biomolecules on the response of the proposed sensor were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the F 560/F 410 value displayed negligible change after adding of the various substances in ratiometric sensor. Note that the concentration of AA and Cys was lower than that of GSH in the selectivity test. This is because the contents of Cys and AA are μM levels in biological environments, which are much lower than that of GSH (mM level) [54,68]. Cys and AA could cause negligible change in F560 /F410 value at μM levels, which is consistent with those in the previous reported literature for the detection of GSH [69,70]. Thus, in this work, the concentrations of Cys and AA for the selectivity study were set to 10 μM, while that of GSH was set to 0.1 mM. Hence, Cys and AA have slight influence for GSH detection, and the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor displayed good selectivity for the detection of GSH in biological environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential of the proposed sensor for detecting GSH in real samples, three human serum samples and three GSH tablets were subjected to GSH detection. To demonstrate the accuracy of this method, the standard OPA based-fluorescence method was also used to detect GSH in the human serum and GSH tablets [55–57]. As shown in Table S4 and S5, the results for GSH in the human serum and GSH tablets obtained by this method agree well with those by the OPA-based method, suggesting that a credible result was obtained. In addition, the satisfactory recoveries ranged from 97.3 to 109.3% were obtained. Above results indicated a potential application of the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor in clinical diagnosis.

In conclusion, by simply Ag doping of Cu NCs, a simple and facile strategy was proposed to synthesize the highly fluorescent and stable alloy Cu/Ag NCs. On this basis, we developed a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor and demonstrated its potential for sensitively and selectively detecting GSH in biological samples. The new sensor is constructed based on the ratiometric fluorescence technology and couples alloy Cu/Ag NCs, MnO2 nanowires and VB1. The feature of the proposed sensor lies on simultaneously tuning the photoluminescence of alloy Cu/Ag NCs and VB1 by bifunctional MnO2 acting as both a nanozyme catalyst and fluorescence quencher. As nanozyme, the MnO2 can accelerate oxidization of nonfluorescent VB1 by dissolved oxygen to generate fluorescent oxVB1. On the other hand, MnO2 nanowires quench the fluorescence of alloy Cu/Ag NCs through IFE, thus acting as fluorescence quencher. In terms of the unique reaction between GSH and MnO2, a ratiometric fluorescence assay can be constructed for the sensitive and selective detection of GSH. In addition, FL emission of oxVB1 at 410 nm and Cu/Ag NCs at 560 nm can be simultaneously acquired by a single-wavelength excitation at 330 nm. Thus, a ratiometric luminescence signal is obtained by using a single-wavelength excitation mode, simplifying the detection process. Compared with other MnO2-based fluorescent GSH detection methods (Table S1) and nanoclusters-based fluorescent GSH detection methods (Table S2), the proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor promises simplicity, wide linear range (10 nM to 70 μM), high sensitivity (LOD = 6.5 nM), and good selectivity for GSH detection in real biological samples. This study not only provides a simple and facile strategy to obtain Cu/Ag NCs with excellent stability and high quantum yield in aqueous media, but also affords a new idea to fabricate a label-free ratiometric fluorescent platform for biosensing application of metal nanoclusters.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707137), the Key Application and Development Program of Chongqing (cstc2017shmsA0159), Research Funding Project of Yangtze Normal University (2017KYQD23), Young Scientist Research Fund of Yangtze Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.02.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xue Hu received her BS degree from Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications in 2014. She is currently a Ph.D. student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University. Her research interests concern on the development of fluorescent sensors for bioanalysis.

Xidong Liu received his BS from Southwest Normal University in 1986. He is currently a chemistry professor in College of Materials and Chemical Engineering at Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences. His research interests include the development of nanomaterials-based chemical sensors for bioanalysis.

Xiaodan Zhang received her BS and Master's degree from Southwest University in 2008 and 2011. She is currently a faculty in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University. Her research interests concern on the development of fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for bioanalysis.

Haiyan Cao received her BS and Ph.D degree from Southwest University in 2009 and 2014. She is currently a faculty in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Yangtze Normal University. Her research interests concern on the development of luminescent sensors for bioanalysis.

Yuming Huang received his Ph.D degree in 2000 from School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Nanjing University. He is currently working as a professor of chemistry at Southwest University. His research interests include the design and development of fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for bioanalysis.
